content.text: is a Python library that provides lightweight, production-ready primitives for building multi-agent workflows. Unlike heavier frameworks, it centres on four concepts —
, tools, and handoffs — and deliberately keeps orchestration logic in plain Python rather than hiding it behind configuration DSLs.
executes it. Everything in between — tool calls, handoffs, guardrails — is transparent and composable. This section walks through the core API so you can build and run your first agent.
Every agent is an instance of
. Its fields define what the agent knows, what it can do, and what shape its output takes.

from agents import Agent, ModelSettings

agent = Agent(
    name="ResearchAssistant",
    instructions="You are a concise research assistant. Answer in bullet points.",
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    model_settings=ModelSettings(temperature=0.3, max_tokens=1024),
    tools=[],          # list of Tool objects added below
    handoffs=[],       # list of Agent objects this agent may transfer to
    output_type=None,  # None → plain text; a Pydantic model → structured JSON
)
is a frozen dataclass, you clone it with
Human-readable identifier, shown in traces and handoff descriptions
System prompt. Pass a callable to generate dynamic instructions at runtime
Model name or a custom Model implementation
Function tools, file-search, web-search, or computer-use tools
Agents this agent may hand work off to
Pydantic model for structured output; None returns a plain string
Checked before the model sees user input
Checked after the model produces a response
class handles the agentic loop: call the model, execute tool calls, loop until the model emits a final response or

from agents import Agent, Runner

agent = Agent(name="Greeter", instructions="Greet the user warmly.")

result = Runner.run_sync(agent, "Hello!")
print(result.final_output)   # "Hello! It's wonderful to meet you …"

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner

agent = Agent(name="Greeter", instructions="Greet the user warmly.")

async def main():
    result = await Runner.run(agent, "Hello!")
    print(result.final_output)

asyncio.run(main())
is a thin convenience wrapper that calls it inside a new event loop. Prefer

import asyncio
from agents import Agent, Runner

agent = Agent(name="Streamer", instructions="Tell a very short story.")

async def main():
    async with Runner.run_streamed(agent, "Tell me a story.") as stream:
        async for event in stream.stream_events():
            if event.type == "raw_response_event":
                delta = event.data.delta
                if hasattr(delta, "content"):
                    for block in delta.content:
                        if hasattr(block, "text"):
                            print(block.text, end="", flush=True)
    print()

asyncio.run(main())
Scripts, tests, or notebooks — no existing event loop
FastAPI handlers, async CLI, any async context
. It carries the final text or structured output, every message exchanged during the run, and the agent that produced the final turn.

result = Runner.run_sync(agent, "What is 2 + 2?")

print(result.final_output)          # "4"
print(result.last_agent.name)       # "Greeter"
print(len(result.new_messages()))   # number of assistant/tool messages this run added

# Convert to a list suitable for passing as conversation history
history = result.to_input_list()
# history is a list[dict] matching the OpenAI messages schema
call continues the conversation without re-sending the original system prompt:

result1 = Runner.run_sync(agent, "My name is Alice.")
result2 = Runner.run_sync(
    agent,
    result1.to_input_list() + [{"role": "user", "content": "What's my name?"}]
)
print(result2.final_output)  # "Your name is Alice."
becomes a callable tool. The SDK extracts the tool name, description, and JSON Schema from the function's name, docstring, and type annotations automatically.

from agents import Agent, Runner, function_tool

@function_tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Return the current weather for a city."""
    # real implementation would call a weather API
    return f"Sunny, 22 °C in {city}"

@function_tool
def add_numbers(a: float, b: float) -> float:
    """Add two numbers together."""
    return a + b

agent = Agent(
    name="ToolUser",
    instructions="Use tools to answer questions accurately.",
    tools=[get_weather, add_numbers],
)

result = Runner.run_sync(agent, "What's the weather in Paris and what is 7 + 8?")
print(result.final_output)
The model decides which tools to call and in what order. The SDK automatically serialises the return value to a string that gets inserted into the conversation as a tool-result message.
lets you add richer descriptions and constraints to each parameter without changing function signatures.
to a Pydantic model forces the agent to return JSON that matches that schema instead of free text.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from agents import Agent, Runner

class MovieReview(BaseModel):
    title: str
    year: int
    rating: float          # 1.0 – 10.0
    one_line_summary: str

critic = Agent(
    name="MovieCritic",
    instructions="You are a concise movie critic. Return structured reviews.",
    output_type=MovieReview,
)

result = Runner.run_sync(critic, "Review Inception (2010).")
review: MovieReview = result.final_output   # fully typed, validated by Pydantic
print(review.title, review.rating)
is an instance of that model. If the model returns malformed JSON, the SDK retries automatically up to the turn limit.
A run ends when the model emits a final response (no pending tool calls) or when

from agents import Agent, Runner, MaxTurnsExceeded

agent = Agent(name="Worker", instructions="Complete the task step by step.")

try:
    result = Runner.run_sync(agent, "Do something complex.", max_turns=5)
    print(result.final_output)
except MaxTurnsExceeded:
    print("Agent did not finish within the turn limit — increase max_turns or simplify the task")
in the SDK is one round-trip: one model call and all tool calls it triggers in that call. Complex agentic chains involving many sequential tool calls may need a higher
An agent can hand work off to a specialist agent using
. When the model decides the task is better handled by another agent, it emits a special tool call; the SDK automatically switches execution to the target agent.

from agents import Agent, Runner

billing_agent = Agent(
    name="BillingSupport",
    instructions="Handle billing questions, refunds, and invoice queries.",
)

tech_agent = Agent(
    name="TechSupport",
    instructions="Resolve technical issues and explain product features.",
)

triage_agent = Agent(
    name="TriageBot",
    instructions=(
        "You are a triage bot. Route billing questions to BillingSupport "
        "and technical questions to TechSupport. Do not answer yourself."
    ),
    handoffs=[billing_agent, tech_agent],
)

result = Runner.run_sync(triage_agent, "I was charged twice for my subscription.")
print(result.last_agent.name)   # "BillingSupport"
print(result.final_output)
helper when you need to attach a custom description or on-handoff callback:

from agents import handoff

def log_handoff(ctx):
    print(f"Handing off to {ctx.agent.name}")

triage_agent = Agent(
    name="TriageBot",
    instructions="Route questions to the right specialist.",
    handoffs=[
        handoff(billing_agent, on_handoff=log_handoff),
        handoff(tech_agent),
    ],
)

Guardrails run before (input) or after (output) the main model call. They are fast checks — typically using a smaller model or a regex — that can block or modify the conversation without consuming the main model's turn budget.
When a guardrail trips, the SDK raises
. Catch these in your application layer to return a safe error response.

The SDK ships with a built-in tracing layer that records every model call, tool invocation, and handoff as a structured span. Traces are useful for debugging, cost analysis, and building evaluation pipelines.

import agents

# The default processor prints spans to stdout during development.
# In production, swap it for a custom exporter (Langfuse, Braintrust, etc.).
agents.enable_verbose_stdout_logging()

from agents import Agent, Runner
from agents.tracing import get_current_span

agent = Agent(
    name="TracedAgent",
    instructions="Answer the question.",
)

result = Runner.run_sync(agent, "What is the speed of light?")

# The RunResult carries the root span of the completed run
span = result.last_response_id   # opaque ID; use an exporter for structured data
print(result.final_output)

For production use, register a custom span exporter that sends spans to your observability backend. The SDK's tracing API follows the OpenTelemetry span conventions so most OTEL-compatible backends accept it with minimal glue code.
lets you generate the system prompt at runtime — useful for injecting session context, user preferences, or per-request data.

from agents import Agent, Runner, RunContextWrapper

def build_instructions(ctx: RunContextWrapper, agent: Agent) -> str:
    user_name = ctx.context.get("user_name", "friend") if ctx.context else "friend"
    return f"You are a personal assistant for {user_name}. Be warm and concise."

dynamic_agent = Agent(
    name="PersonalAssistant",
    instructions=build_tracing_instructions,  # callable, not a string
)

result = Runner.run_sync(
    dynamic_agent,
    "What should I do today?",
    context={"user_name": "Alice"},
)
print(result.final_output)
(which holds the optional context dict) and the
instance. It must return a string synchronously.
passes arbitrary data through the entire run without threading it through every function signature.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from agents import Agent, Runner, RunContextWrapper, function_tool

@dataclass
class AppContext:
    user_id: str
    tenant: str

@function_tool
def get_user_data(ctx: RunContextWrapper[AppContext]) -> str:
    """Fetch profile data for the current user."""
    app = ctx.context
    return f"user={app.user_id}, tenant={app.tenant}"

agent = Agent(
    name="ContextAwareAgent",
    instructions="Use the get_user_data tool to personalise responses.",
    tools=[get_user_data],
)

result = Runner.run_sync(
    agent,
    "Tell me about my account.",
    context=AppContext(user_id="u-123", tenant="acme"),
)
print(result.final_output)
Context is typed with a generic parameter —
— so tools that declare the type parameter get full type-checker support inside their function body.
The SDK raises typed exceptions for predictable failure modes. Catch them to build robust application logic.
Increase max_turns or split into smaller tasks
An input guardrail flagged the request
Return a safe rejection message to the user
An output guardrail flagged the response
Retry with adjusted instructions or reject
Model returned unparseable output multiple times
Log and surface to a human for review
Fix the Agent definition — not a runtime error
The SDK allows the model to call multiple tools in a single turn when they are independent. This is enabled by default on models that support it. Set
to disable it if tool calls must be sequential.
objects are stateless and immutable — create them once at module load time and reuse them across requests. Each call to

# Module-level singletons — no per-request overhead
_summariser = Agent(
    name="Summariser",
    instructions="Summarise the provided text in three bullet points.",
)

async def summarise(text: str) -> str:
    result = await Runner.run(_summariser, text)
    return result.final_output

from agents import Agent, Runner

chat_agent = Agent(name="ChatBot", instructions="Be helpful and concise.")

history = []   # starts empty; grows with each turn

while True:
    user_input = input("You: ")
    if user_input.lower() == "quit":
        break
    result = Runner.run_sync(chat_agent, history + [{"role": "user", "content": user_input}])
    history = result.to_input_list()   # capture full history for next turn
    print(f"Bot: {result.final_output}")
